coredata.dc:title: A solvent-free gaseous detonation approach for converting benzoic acid into graphene quantum dots within milliseconds
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Honghao
He, Chuang
Li, Xiaojie
Zhao, Tiejun
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in high reproducibility are synthesized by a gaseous detonation method which converts untreated benzoic acid (BA) precursor into powdered GODs without complicated post-treatment in 3–5 ms. The as-prepared GQDs resulting in an absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 21.5% exhibit dual-wavelength-independent emission with the maximum PL emission at 431 nm and 460 nm under excitation maximum at 300 nm dispersed in ethyl alcohol. Based on surface structure, photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of GQDs and the characteristics of gaseous detonation, the formation mechanism of GQDs is speculated. Moreover, further study regarding the optical properties of reducing GQDs (R-GQDs) and thermal annealing GQDs (A-GQDs), it is possible to reveal PL mechanism of GQDs. This research not only provides a one-pot solvent-free route to rapid and large-scale preparation of GQDs, but also accounts for the hypothetic formation and PL mechanism of the obtained GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Gaseous detonation
Formation mechanism
Photoluminescence mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of reducing GQDs (R-GQDs) and thermal annealing GQDs (A-GQDs)
Morphology and composition of the GQDs
The mechanism of synthesis and PL for GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The project was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11672068 , 10872044 , and 11672067 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images at different magnifications: (a) 50 nm (with the size distribution, inset) and (b) 10 nm (with the crystal lattice of (100) facet (upper) and the FFT patterns (lower), inset); (c) AFM image; (d) height profile along the white line in the AFM image.
(a) FT-IR spectra of BA and GQDs; (b) XPS survey spectrum of GQDs; (c) C1s spectrum of GQDs; (d) C1s spectrum of GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of BA and GQDs (with optical photographs of CQDs dispersed in ethyl alcohol taken under sunlight light and 365 nm UV light, inset); (b) PL spectra of CQDs and BA; (c) PL intensity of GQDs as the function of irritation time.
The preparation and growth mechanism of GQDs.

(a) the PL behaviors of GQDs at different pH values; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of R-GQDs and A-GQDs; (c) PL spectra of R-GQDs and A-GQDs (Green and blue line correspond to R-GQDs and A-GQDs, respectively). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Proposed PL emission mechanisms at different stages. (a) Electronic transition diagram of GQDs; (b) Electronic transition diagram of R-GQDs or A-GQDs; (c) Structural model of GQDs; (d) Structural model of R-GQDs or A-GQDs.

head.ce:title: A solvent-free gaseous detonation approach for converting benzoic acid into graphene quantum dots within milliseconds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Structural Analysis for Industrial Equipment, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, Liaoning 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Structural Analysis for Industrial Equipment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in high reproducibility are synthesized by a gaseous detonation method which converts untreated benzoic acid (BA) precursor into powdered GODs without complicated post-treatment in 3–5 ms. The as-prepared GQDs resulting in an absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 21.5% exhibit dual-wavelength-independent emission with the maximum PL emission at 431 nm and 460 nm under excitation maximum at 300 nm dispersed in ethyl alcohol. Based on surface structure, photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of GQDs and the characteristics of gaseous detonation, the formation mechanism of GQDs is speculated. Moreover, further study regarding the optical properties of reducing GQDs (R-GQDs) and thermal annealing GQDs (A-GQDs), it is possible to reveal PL mechanism of GQDs. This research not only provides a one-pot solvent-free route to rapid and large-scale preparation of GQDs, but also accounts for the hypothetic formation and PL mechanism of the obtained GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), being a dazzling star in the nanocarbon family, are graphene nanosheers of less than 100 nm size [1]. The superior properties, such as resistance to photo-bleaching [2], good biocompatibility [3–6], and tunable luminescence [6–8] make them popular in fluorescent probes [9, 10], bio-imaging [6, 11–13], and optoelectronic devices [14, 15]. Generally, the synthetic approaches of GQDs fall into top-down and bottom-up [16, 17]. The top-down approaches involve cutting bulk graphene sources into GQDs via hydrothermal [8] or solvothermal method [13], electrochemical approach [18], microwave-assisted route [12], ultrasonic shearing [19], and acid oxidation [20], while bottom-up approaches are achieved using organic molecules to synthesize graphene moieties with a conjugated structure through microwave synthesis [2, 15] or solution chemistry [21, 22]. So far, the microwave synthesis is popular by virtue of simple synthetic scheme, time-saving process and cheap precursors without acid-free treatment [2, 15]. However, the limitations of microwave synthesis are not to be sneezed at, such as a little production and complicated post-treatment to obtain powdered GODs. Recently, some new synthetic approaches such as high-energy ball milling [23] and chemical vapor deposition [24] have been reported. Compared with the microwave synthesis, new synthetic approaches have some successes in production or post-treatment, but the disadvantages are evident such as time-consuming process and low yield. Therefore, a facile, rapid and efficient synthetic approach of GQDs is still highly desirable.

Gaseous detonation, which uses combustible gas and oxygen as explosion sources to generate detonation wave that gives rise to a complex and fast series of chemical reactions in the precursors after ignition, has been successfully applied in materials synthesis, such as carbon nanostructure materials [25, 26] and metal oxides [27, 28]. The gaseous detonation approach can directly produce powdered nanomaterials on a large scale within milliseconds using untreated precursors [26]. As far as we know, the synthesis of GQDs by gaseous detonation has not been reported.

Herein, we report a one-pot gaseous detonation approach to prepare GQDs with untreated BA as the carbon source and hydrogen and oxygen as the explosion source. It is effortless to synthesize powdery GQDs directly within milliseconds by the gaseous detonation approach which is much faster than that of the hitherto reported synthetic method of GQDs. More importantly, through controlling detonation parameters, the results about GQDs are in high reproducibility. The obtained GQDs resulting in an absolute PLQY of 21.5% exhibit dual-wavelength-independent emission with maximum PL emission at 431 nm and 460 nm under excitation maximum at 300 nm. Moreover, stable PL behaviors within pH 3.0–14.0 and high resistance to photo-bleaching are observed. Based on surface structure, PL behaviors of GQDs and the characteristics of gaseous detonation, the formation mechanism of GQDs is speculated. Furthermore, further study regarding the optical properties of R-GQDs and A-GQDs, we try to reveal the possible PL mechanism of GQDs.

BA, absolute ethyl alcohol and NaBH4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen and oxygen with the purity of 99.999% were purchased from Dalian Special Gases Co. (Dalian, China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

A self-made detonation titanium tube, which could achieve thermoregulation by an oil heater around the tube, was used to carry out the experiment. The tube, being a cylinder container with inner diameter of 100 mm, length of 1100 mm and volume of 8.6 L, which could be sealed by flange plates with rubber ring, possessed vacuum-pumping system, temperature-controlling system, pressure-monitoring system and high-energy-igniting system. A vacuum pump, which connected the detonation tube via an exhaust pipe, was the main part of the vacuum-pumping system. The temperature-controlling system could adjust the heating temperature up to 473.15 K while the pressure-monitoring system was able to monitor the internal pressure in real time. The high-energy-igniting system contained a high-energy igniter located the end of the detonation tube which could generate over 40 J ignition energy to detonate the combustible gas mixture. Fig. 1 exhibits the schematic of the detonation tube.

GQDs were prepared as follows: BA (4 g) as carbon source was tiled in the detonation tube. Secondly, the flange plates and vacuum-pumping system were used to seal and vacuumize the tube, respectively. Then, the temperature control system was taken advantage of to heat the gaseous detonation tube to 393.15 K. The amount of sublimation of BA could be determined by observing the variation of the pressure-monitoring system. Hydrogen and oxygen were pumped into the tube, respectively. The molar ratio of BA, hydrogen and oxygen was 3:8:8. It took 2–3 min to blend the gases spontaneously. The pressure was controlled below 0.75 atm. Finally, the high-energy igniter was made use of to detonate the gas mixture. Consequently, the BA was transformed into powdery GQDs within 3–5 ms under the effect of detonation of gas mixture. After cooling down to 333.15 K, black powdered GQDs (0.21 g) in the detonation tube were collected. The as-obtained GQDs didn't conduct any kind of purification. The time spanning the whole experiment could be controlled within 60 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of reducing GQDs (R-GQDs) and thermal annealing GQDs (A-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The as-obtained GQDs (0.01 g) were dissolved in the ethanol solution (10 mL). Then the distilled water (10 mL) and Sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 0.05 g) were added to GQDs ethanol solution under vigorous stirring at room temperature for overnight. The R-GQDs were obtained by drying.
The A-GQDs were obtained using a tube furnace to heat the as-obtained GQDs (0.01 g) put in a quartz boat at 723.15 K for 15 min under nitrogen atmosphere.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded on a Tecnai F30 field-emission electron microscope operating at 300 kV (FEI, USA). The test sample for TEM was prepared as follows: GQDs were diluted in absolute ethyl alcohol and the suspension dropped on carbon-coated copper grid by evaporation in the air. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were measured in the tapping mode on a MultiMode3D atomic force microscopy with a tip type of RTESP (Veeco, USA). The test sample for AFM was prepared as follows: GQDs ethanol solution was spotted onto a fresh silicon slice and dried in air. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were confirmed by an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku D/MAX 2400, Japan) using Cu Kα as excitation source (λ = 0.15406 nm) with 6°/min scanning speed. Raman spectra were performed using an excitation wavelength at 532 nm by XploRA Raman spectrometer (Horiba Co., France). The as-prepared powdery GQDs without any treatment were used for Raman test. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB 250, USA) with an Al Kα 280 eV radiation source and the C1s peak at 284.8 eV as a binding energy calibration. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer Lambda 750, USA) with a 1.0 cm quartz cell. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were characterized by a Nicolet-6700 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA) from 500 to 4000 cm−1 using KBr pressed disks. PL spectra were recorded by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) employing a 1 cm quartz cell at room temperature. The absolute PLQY of the GQDs was obtained using the Quantaurus-QY Instrument (Hamamatsu Photonics CO. Ltd., Japan). GQDs ethanol solution diluted to the absorbance of 0.1 was prepared for absolute PLQY test. The pH values were acquired employing a PB-10 pH-meter (Beijing Sartorius Instruments Co. Ltd., China). The thermal annealing operation was achieved by a tube furnace (Hefei Kejing Materials Technology Co. Ltd., China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and composition of the GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a shows the morphology of GQDs as characterized by HRTEM, which can be seen to have a uniform dispersion without aggregation. The inset in Fig. 2a illustrates a size distribution of GQDs within the range of 1.6–3.8 nm and an average of ca. 2.5 nm by counting 150 particles. A crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to (100) lattice spacing of graphite [29] can be observed from Fig. 2b in some GQDs. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns (lower inset of Fig. 2b) of GQDs also confirm crystalline structure. AFM analyses reveal a topographical height in the range of 0.48–1.12 nm for the GQDs (Fig. 2c and d), indicating that 1–2 graphene layers GQDs are obtained [30].

XRD patterns (Fig. 3a) show a broad peak centered at around 24.3° corresponding to the graphite (002) plane which confirms a good degree of crystallization of GQDs [11]. In addition, due to the small size of the GQDs, the peak at 24.3° is broad [15]. Interestingly, demonstrating the graphite (100) plane of 0.21 nm lattice spacing reflected by the peak at 42.9° can be clearly observed, which further indicates well-organized sp2-hybridized structure of the GQDs. Another evidence for the conclusions drawn from HRTEM and AFM images is provided by Raman spectrum (Fig. 3b), which exhibits D band at 1326 cm−1 and G band at 1582 cm−1, attributing to disordered sp3 defects and the sp2 CC bond, respectively [31, 32]. The I D/I G ratio is 1.05, which suggests good crystallinity of the CQDs.

Fig. 4a shows the FT-IR spectra of BA and GQDs. Because of the structural changes caused by gaseous detonation, two distinct new bonds at 1098 cm−1 and 3427 cm−1 appear in GQDs, which belong to COC and OH [33, 34], respectively. At the same time, the peaks at 1384 cm−1 and 2922 cm−1 of GQDs prove the existence of CH [15]. In addition, hydrophobicity but solubility in alcoholic medium implies a higher proportion of CH than OH. The band of aromatic CC skeletal vibration (1603 cm−1) of BA shifts to a higher frequency at 1627 cm−1 in GQDs [35]. The stretching vibration band of carboxyl (1292 cm−1 and 1704 cm−1) of BA disappears, which demonstrates that there is so limited carboxyl in GQDs that FT-IR fail to detect it. XPS spectra once again testify the conclusions from FT-IR spectra. The full range XPS (Fig. 4b) spectra of the GQDs involve C1s peak (284 eV) and O1s peak (532 eV). The molar ratio of C and O in GQDs is about 4.61 which is higher than that of BA (3.50). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) exhibits a typical peak at 284.6 eV, which should be ascribed to sp2 carbon [2, 24] with a fraction of 56.23%. In addition, sp3 carbons include the CC/CH peak at 285.2 eV (37.09%) [15], a COC/COH peak at 286.6 eV (3.98%) [12] and a COOH peak at 289.0 eV (2.70%) [18, 36]. The O1s peak splits into two peaks at 532.4 eV and 533.1 eV (Fig. 4d), corresponding to COC/COH and COOH [12, 37]. There are lots of evidences from FT-IR and XPS spectra to support the fact that the GQDs contain a mixture of sp2 and sp3 bonding.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 5a) exhibits two strong absorption bands at 287 nm and 300 nm which are derived from π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, which is similar to that of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) [38], and three weak absorption bands at 363 nm, 382 nm and 429 nm which are assigned to n–π* absorption of defect state and oxygen-containing functional groups [15, 36]. The GQDs dispersed in ethyl alcohol show yellow color exposure to sunlight and emit a bright blue fluorescence excited with 365 nm UV light, as illustrated in the inset in Fig. 5a. The GQDs display dual-wavelength-independent emission and have the maximum dual PL emission at 431 nm and 460 nm under excitation maximum at 300 nm that agree with the strongest adsorption band of UV–vis.

The excitation-independent PL could be explained by the GQDs having identical absorption structures and luminescent centers [39]. It should be noted that BA displays a strong UV absorption 225 nm and a weak peak at 272 nm (Fig. 5a) and there is little PL emission over 400 nm on excitation at 300 nm (Fig. 5b). The absolute PLQY of GQDs which only contain elements of hydrogen, carbon, and oxygen is 21.5%, which is higher than previous reports [15, 23]. The result that the PL intensity of GQDs remains almost unchanged under irradiation using a xenon lamp for 2 h shown in Fig. 5c indicates excellent photo-stability of the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of synthesis and PL for GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: All changes mentioned above prove that the gaseous detonation approach affects the morphology, composition, and optical properties of the GQDs. There are two fundamental to be explained. 1) How is the BA converted into GQDs during the detonation? 2) What's the cause of the strong blue dual-wavelength-independent emission of the GQDs?

Owing to the difficulties in tracking and characterizing the complicated reactions, the formation mechanism of GQDs using the bottom-up method is very challenging [40]. In this study, the mechanism of synthesis was investigated based on surface structure, PL behaviors of GQDs and the characteristics of gaseous detonation. The Zeldovich-von Neumann-Döring (ZND) model of detonation (Fig. 6 ) consisting of three regions (reactants, reaction zone, and products) is used to explain the formation of GQDs.

Though the PL mechanism of GQDs is not fully understood, a possible explanation could be given based on the structure characteristics and optical properties of GQDs, R-GQDs and A-GQDs. Owing to the same PL property (Fig. 5b) and similar response to the pH stimulation (Fig. 9a), the two fluorescence peaks of GQDs should share the same PL mechanism. That is small sp2 isolated within the sp3 carbon network clusters dominate the PL emission [44], while the surface states that are oxygen containing functional groups and defects localized on edge of sp2 clusters play a supplementary role in the GQDs emission. It means that the electron-hole (e-h) pairs recombination mainly occurs in isolated small sp2, but not all. The diagram of electronic transition for GQDs is displayed in Fig. 10a. The PL mechanism is in accordance with the previous studies that at high synthesis temperatures, aromatic domains dominate the PL [45, 46]. The PL mechanism is supported by the following aspects. Firstly, from the formation mechanism of GQDs, it's clear that every GQD contains multiple sp2 subdomains which serve as the origin of fluorescence. At the same time, employing DFT and time-dependent DFT, the pristine GQDs of ~2 nm in size is red-fluorescent [42, 43]. The fact that the as-prepared GQDs possess an average of ca. 2.5 nm but emit a blue fluorescence implies there is no complete sp2 in as-prepared GQDs and sp2 subdomains containing 7–9 benzene rings embedded within sp3 matrix are the main part of GQDs. The XPS result of GQDs also confirms sp2 carbon only takes up a fraction of 56.23%. The five different absorption bands from UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs manifest two types of electron transition (sp2 clusters and surface states). The asymmetrical PL spectra with band tail over 500 nm obviously show the surface state also emit fluorescence but a few [47]. What's more, as shown in Fig. 9a, the PL behaviors of GQDs immersed in different pH circumstances under excitation maximum at 300 nm are measured. Due to the protonation based on the formation of the electron-donor/ acceptor complex between the delocalized π-electron system with H+, the GQDs exhibit excellent alkali-resistivity and relative stability within a wide range of pH 3.0–14.0 [8]. Nearly 20% fluorescence decay under strong alkali attributes to the PL quench from the surface states. In addition, the dual-wavelength emission may be ascribed to two kinds of sp2 subdomains and the difference between them may be edge structure or the number of benzene rings.

The optical properties of R-GQDs and A-GQDs were used to further validate PL mechanism. It should be noted that the optimal excitation of GQDs, R-GQDs and A-GQDs is same as the maximum absorption. The result confirms that π-conjugated electron structure of the sp2 subdomain governs the excitation [48]. The absolute PLQY of R-GQDs and A-GQDs drop to 1.7% and 0.1% at 300 nm excitation, respectively. The excitation/emission maximum of R-GQDs and A-GQDs (Fig. 9c) is same at 280/310 nm which has a blue shift in comparison with the PL spectrum of GQDs consistent with previous reports [40, 44]. Moreover, the fact that there is no band tail in symmetrical PL spectra of R-GQDs and A-GQDs indicates the surface states have no contribution to fluorescence. The UV–vis absorption spectra of R-GQDs and A-GQDs (Fig. 9b) only have one weak peak at 275 nm which is attributed to π–π* transition of sp2. That further confirms the surface state no longer emit fluorescence in the R-GQDs and A-GQDs.

These results are consistent with our hypothesis of PL mechanism. As shown in Fig. 10, we propose our structural model for GQDs at different stages to explain the optical results. In Fig. 10c, the GQDs prepared by gaseous detonation contain a maximum of localized sp2 subdomains within the insulating sp3 matrix, and the energetic coupling between these subdomains remains negligibly small, thereby the GQDs have a high absolute PLQY [44]. The treatment by reduction with NaBH4 or thermal annealing at 723.15 K fails to make initially present sp2 subdomains increase in size that have been experimentally demonstrated [44, 49, 50], but leads to percolation between the initially present sp2 subdomains via growth of new smaller sp2 clusters [44, 47], as shown in Fig. 10d. Using the particle-in-a-box argument, it can be simply inferred that the blue PL from the sp2 subdomains shown in Fig. 10b is likely to be significantly quenched as shown in Fig. 9c [44]. Meanwhile, the surface states of GQDs are changed by the reduction or annealing. Not only are oxygen-containing functional groups localized on edge of sp2 subdomains mainly removed, but also the defect state on the surface is restored. As a result, emission peaks and absorption peaks from surface states of R-GQDs and A-GQDs have disappeared. Furthermore, the emission maximum of R-GQDs and A-GQDs at 310 nm may be put down to the reason that newly formed smaller sp2 clusters only including 1–2 benzene rings as displayed in the purple area in Fig. 10d, which are not involved in interconnectivity and remain isolated in sp3 matrix, are responsible for fluorescence below 400 nm. In a word, by reduction or thermal annealing, the interconnectivity of the localized sp2 subdomains is enhanced as shown in the irregular the black area in Fig. 10c, and the surface states of GQDs are changed, which facilitate transport of excitons to nonradiative recombination sites, and consequently quench PL.

GQDs with highly fluorescent have been prepared via a facile one-step approach. The gaseous detonation preparation strategy not only avoids the use of any solvent, but also achieves the direct synthesis of powdery GQDs within milliseconds. The characteristics of using cheap precursors without acid-free treatment, large-scale fabrication and high reproducibility make gaseous detonation potential and promising for industrial production of GQDs. The properties including stable PL behaviors within pH 3.0–14.0 and high resistance to photo-bleaching enable the obtained GQDs to show potential in the field of bio-sensing and white-light emitting diodes. What's more, based on surface structure, PL behaviors of GQDs and the characteristics of gaseous detonation, we speculate the formation mechanism of GQDs. That is BA goes through a sequential DCDP approach to form PAHs (namely sp2 subdomains), subsequently a series of collisions among PAHs, oxygen-containing functional groups and massive free carbons agglomerate into GQDs. Last but not least, further study regarding the optical properties of R-GQDs and A-GQDs, it is possible to reveal PL mechanism of GQDs. That is small sp2 isolated within the sp3 carbon network clusters dominate the PL emission, while the surface states that are oxygen containing functional groups and defects localized in edge of sp2 clusters play a supplementary role in the GQDs emission.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11672068, 10872044, and 11672067).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2018.06.008.
